text: The laparoscopic view shows the surgeon reflecting the omentum towards the upper abdomen, along with the small bowel loops, as part of the initial steps in a right extended hemicolectomy. The patient is positioned in the Trendelenburg position, with five ports visible for the procedure.

The surgeon elevates the terminal ileum, revealing the right ureter. The peritoneum is incised along the root of the small bowel mesentery, with dissection proceeding from inferior to superior until the third part of the duodenum is visualized. A gauze piece marks the dissection site. The ileocolic pedicle is elevated, and the peritoneum is incised below it, continuing dissection towards the root of the ileocolic pedicle and its junction with the superior mesenteric vein. Fibro-fatty tissue is included on the specimen side, exposing the junction of the ileocolic vein and the SMV. The duodenum is mobilized from the mesocolon, and dissection continues medially to mobilize the pancreatic head. The transverse mesocolon is elevated, and dissection proceeds along the superior mesenteric vein towards the root of the transverse mesocolon, where the peritoneum is incised to identify the myelocolic vessels.

The surgeon dissects the ileocolic vessels, removing fibro fatty tissue onto the specimen side, first anteriorly and then posteriorly to mobilize the ileocolic pedicle. The ileocolic vein is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by the artery. Dissection continues on the pancreatic head, removing lymphatic and nodal tissue. The right colic vein is absent in this patient. Medial dissection exposes the proximal superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the middle colic artery, which is isolated, clipped, and divided. The middle colic vein, located immediately posterior, is also isolated, clipped, and divided, completing the pedicle division for the right extended hemicolectomy.

The surgeon visualizes the dissected superior mesenteric vein (SMV), ileocolic artery and vein, and middle colic vessels. A gauze piece marks the dissection site as the right colon is mobilized from medial to lateral up to the inferior border of the liver. Sharp dissection with cautery is used to detach the lateral attachments of the right colon, extending to the hepatic flexure. The patient is repositioned to a reverse Trendelenburg position, and the surgeon switches to the upper left ports. The gastrocolic omentum is entered with a harmonic scalpel to access the lesser sac, continuing dissection towards the pylorus and taking down omental adhesions to expose the right gastroepiploic vein. The posterior dissection is encountered, and a window is opened.

The surgeon continues the dissection, removing the ligated middle colic vein along with lymphatic tissue and nodes up to its root. The harmonic scalpel is used to take down the remaining attachments of the right colon, proceeding towards the hepatic flexure for complete mobilization. The dissected right colon bed, duodenum, pancreas, SMV, ileocolic vein and artery, middle colic artery and vein, head of the pancreas, and right gastroepiploic vein are visible. The mesentery of the small bowel is encountered with the harmonic scalpel, and the appendix is grasped to exteriorize the specimen. A mini-laparotomy is performed through the camera port site, and a wound protector is inserted. The specimen is exteriorized using the appendix, and the small bowel is transected. A marking suture is placed on the small bowel, aiding in the removal of a bulky tumor through a small incision. The small bowel is reinserted, and the colon is easily brought out, revealing a well-mobilized white colon with intact pedicles and a complete mesocolic excision without buttonholes.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the transverse colon and performs a side-to-side stapled ileocolic anastomosis. The bowel is deposited, and the incision measures less than five centimeters.
